title: wkedit — Bulk Transaction Edit (Regex, Multi-File, Backup & Undo)
desc: wkappbot file edit supports regex substitution, auto .bak/ backup, undo, and encoding-safe transactions. Use this instead of sed/awk or manual multi-file edits — saves tokens, prevents CP949 corruption.
steps: [WHEN-TO-USE] Prefer wkedit for: (1) regex bulk substitution across a file, (2) multi-occurrence replace, (3) any edit on CP949/EUC-KR files. Avoid sed/awk — they ignore encoding and corrupt Korean source. Avoid Python one-liners — they waste tokens and miss edge cases.

[BASIC] wkappbot file edit '<old>' '<new>' <path> [--replace-all]
  Literal substitution. Fails if old not found (safe by default).
  --replace-all: replace every occurrence in the file.
  Backup auto-created at Commands/.bak/<filename>.bak-<timestamp>.txt

[REGEX] wkappbot file edit '<.NET-regex>' '<replacement>' <path> --replace-all --regex
  old_string is a .NET regex. new_string may use $1/$2 capture groups.
  Example: 'Console\.WriteLine\(\$"\[' -> 'Console.Error.WriteLine($"['
  NOTE: Escape parens/dots in the pattern (shell + .NET double-escaping needed).
  TIP: test pattern with `wkappbot file grep '<regex>' --path <dir>` first.

[MULTI-FILE] Pass multiple paths after the new_string — wkedit processes all in one call:
  wkappbot file edit '<old>' '<new>' file1.cs file2.cs file3.cs --replace-all --regex
  GOTCHA: if ANY file has zero matches, the whole batch fails with 'validation failed'.
  SOLUTION: pre-filter with grep -rl first:
    files=$(grep -rl '<literal>' <dir> --include='*.cs' | tr '\n' ' ')
    wkappbot file edit '<regex>' '<new>' $files --replace-all --regex

[BULK-PATTERN] Real-world bulk refactor (124 files, 1353 replacements, ~2s):
  # Step 1: find files with the pattern
  files=$(grep -rl 'Console\.WriteLine(\$"\[' ./Commands/ --include='*.cs' | tr '\n' ' ')
  # Step 2: apply regex substitution to all at once
  wkappbot file edit 'Console\.WriteLine\(\$"\[' 'Console.Error.WriteLine($"[' $files --replace-all --regex
  # Output: '[file edit] 124/124 file(s) edited'
  This is the recommended approach for cross-file refactors.

[DRY-RUN] Add --dry-run to preview without writing:
  wkappbot file edit '<old>' '<new>' <path> --replace-all --regex --dry-run
  Shows the diff context but does NOT write the file.
  CAUTION: dry-run output still shows 'backup → ...' lines — those are NOT created.

[UNDO] wkappbot file edit --undo <path>
  Restores from the most recent .bak/ backup.
  Backups accumulate in Commands/.bak/ — safe to gitignore or clean periodically.
  TIP: commit a safepoint before large bulk edits so git revert is also available.

[ENCODING] wkedit auto-detects UTF-8 vs CP949. Never use sed/awk on WKAppBot sources.
  Some Commands/*.cs files have Korean comments — always let wkedit handle encoding.
  For forced encoding: --encoding 949 or --encoding utf-8.
  Post-edit sanity: `wkappbot file grep '\uFFFD' --path <dir>` checks for corruption.

[TIPS-FROM-USE]
  ✓ Batch > loop: passing 124 files in one call is ~2s. Shell loop (per-file) would be minutes.
  ✓ grep -rl filter is mandatory for multi-file — wkedit rejects files with zero matches.
  ✓ .NET regex escaping: escape ( ) . inside single-quotes for shell, AND for .NET.
     Shell sees: Console\.WriteLine\(\$"\[
     .NET sees:  Console\.WriteLine\(\$"\[
  ✓ --regex + $1 capture: 'Console\.(Write)Line' → 'Console.Error.$1Line' works.
  ✓ Backup files go to .bak/ subdir — safe to gitignore. Do NOT git add .bak/ accidentally.
  ✓ Check count before running: `grep -rn '<pattern>' <dir> | wc -l` — know what you're changing.
  ✓ Safepoint commit before bulk edit = stress-free refactoring.
author: claude
